agent1_raw: Agent 1 analysis: The central problem with this evaluation is that the label 'interpreter was joined' reflects what dispatchers did under prior practice, not whether interpretation was genuinely necessary. Dispatcher teams had different default behaviors — some always called interpreters early, others deferred to bilingual staff. A model trained on this label learns to predict the old policy, not the underlying communication need. When deployed to change the policy (by allowing delays), the model's training target becomes irrelevant to the decision. Additionally, the evaluation presents only aggregate metrics without separating call types. Delaying interpretation on a chest-pain or domestic violence call has different risk than delaying it on a property dispute, but the evaluation cannot distinguish these. The override rule is a useful safeguard but does not validate the model's performance for the non-override population.
agent2_raw: Agent 2 analysis: Three concerns stand out. First, the label is policy-dependent: it captures what happened under the incumbent practice, not what would have happened under a delayed-connection regime. This is the most fundamental issue — the model cannot be validated for a policy it has never operated under. Second, the evaluation is aggregate-only: without stratification by call acuity and type, the headline metrics cannot establish safety for high-stakes call categories. Third, the override rule, while beneficial, operates on proxies (distress cues, location confirmation) that may not capture all high-acuity situations. The retrospective evidence is insufficient to justify deployment as framed. A prospective pilot, properly scoped with independent communication-failure adjudication, would be the minimum requirement.
agent3_raw: Agent 3 analysis: The proposed deployment asks a different question than the retrospective study answers. The study measures whether historical labels (policy-driven) are predictable. The deployment asks whether delaying connection for low-score calls will result in acceptable communication outcomes. These are not the same question, and the historical data cannot answer the deployment question because the counterfactual (what happens when you delay an interpreter) was never observed at scale. Separately, the aggregate precision/recall metrics do not differentiate between call types where the cost of a miss varies dramatically. The absence of stratified analysis by call type is a critical gap. The override rule provides partial protection but is not a substitute for valid prospective evidence.
synthesis_raw: All three agents converge on two core issues: (1) the label is policy-driven and cannot validate a policy-change decision; (2) aggregate metrics hide asymmetric error costs across call types. Agent 1 and Agent 3 both identify the fundamental circularity of using behavior-driven labels to justify changing the behavior. Agent 2 adds the point that the override rule relies on proxies that may miss some high-acuity calls. No agent raised empirically resolvable disagreements — all converge on critique_wins. ETD constraint check: the issues are not empirically resolvable from existing retrospective data; the required evidence is a prospective pilot with independent communication-failure adjudication.
all_issues_raised: Policy-driven labels cannot validate a policy-change decision
Aggregate metrics hide asymmetric error costs across call types
Override rule relies on proxies that may miss some high-acuity calls
Counterfactual (delayed interpreter) was never observed at scale
